<!--
---
title: "Research"
description: "Peer-reviewed research by Michael F. Joseph on great power politics, national security, secrecy, and technology, including his book The Origins of Great Power Rivalries (Cambridge University Press)."
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---
--> 


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
source("R/render-pubs.R")
```

## Book:  The Origins of Great Power Rivalries

::: {.book-press}
Cambridge University Press · 2026
:::

:::::: book-feature
::: book-left
![](images/book/cover-published.jpg){.book-cover
fig-alt="The Origins of Great Power Rivalries — book cover"}

```{=html}
<a class="book-video-thumb" href="#book-video-modal" role="button" aria-label="Play video summary" onclick="openBookVideo(event); return false;">
  <img src="https://i.ytimg.com/vi/cteDEjlMla8/hqdefault.jpg" alt="Video summary thumbnail" loading="lazy">
  <span class="book-video-play" aria-hidden="true"></span>
  <span class="book-video-cap">Video summary</span>
</a>
<dialog id="book-video-modal" class="video-modal">
  <button type="button" class="video-modal-close" onclick="closeBookVideo()" aria-label="Close video">&times;</button>
  <div class="video-modal-frame" id="book-video-frame"></div>
</dialog>
<script>
function openBookVideo(e){ if(e){ e.preventDefault(); }
  document.getElementById('book-video-frame').innerHTML =
    '<iframe src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/cteDEjlMla8?autoplay=1&rel=0" title="Video summary" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>';
  var d = document.getElementById('book-video-modal');
  if (d.showModal) { d.showModal(); } else { d.setAttribute('open',''); }
}
function closeBookVideo(){
  var d = document.getElementById('book-video-modal');
  if (d.close) { d.close(); } else { d.removeAttribute('open'); }
  document.getElementById('book-video-frame').innerHTML = '';
}
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function(){
  var d = document.getElementById('book-video-modal');
  if (!d) return;
  d.addEventListener('click', function(ev){ if (ev.target === d) { closeBookVideo(); } });
  d.addEventListener('close', function(){ document.getElementById('book-video-frame').innerHTML = ''; });
});
</script>
```
:::

:::: book-right
::: book-endorse

<div class="blurb"><p class="q">“In <i>The Origins of Great Power Rivalries</i>, Michael F. Joseph *cogently explains why status quo states often take ‘too long’ to respond to rising challengers.* Joseph theorizes that declining powers judge the challenger’s actions against a historically informed and articulated principle announced by the latter: only when actions deviate from this principle does the defender learn the challenger has more expansive goals and turns to competition. Joseph explains not only past cases but *sheds important light on contemporary US–China relations.*”</p><p class="who">David A. Lake<span>Distinguished Professor of the Graduate Division, UC San Diego</span></p></div>

<div class="blurb"><p class="q">“Michael F. Joseph sheds valuable new light on a *central question of international relations theory.* He explains that understanding why an adversary wants to expand enables a state to make calibrated decisions about how intensely to compete or cooperate. Joseph supports his nuanced theory with an *impressive array of empirical tests*. <i>The Origins of Great Power Rivalries</i> makes a *major contribution to IR theory.* ”</p><p class="who">Charles Glaser<span>Senior Fellow, Security Studies Program, MIT</span></p></div>

<div class="blurb"><p class="q">“The book makes an *important contribution to the theory of international relations.* One explanation of why Britain decided to fight Hitler when he took the rest of Czechoslovakia is that it did not fit with a nationalist German program but signaled more extensive ambitions. *Joseph formalizes and generalizes this core insight* and shows that *it applies in a number of other important cases over the past 200 years of international history.*”</p><p class="who">Andrew H. Kydd<span>Professor of International Relations, University of Wisconsin</span></p></div>

:::
::::

::: book-actions

<details class="book-cta">
<summary>Summary</summary>

::: book-summary
I advance a comprehensive rationalist explanation for why great powers
sometime forge lasting friendships and other times engage in catastrophic
competition. In an important departure from traditional realist theory, I argue
that countries are motivated by distinct principles - normative values that
shape foreign policy beyond simple security concerns. I develop a strategic
theory that allows a rising power to value distinct normative principles. It
shows that rational status quo powers draw qualitative inferences about their
rivals' intentions by examining the historical context of their demands, not
just military capabilities; explaining puzzling threat estimates that unevenly
interpret a rival's violent actions; and how costless diplomacy functions when
rising powers emerge on the scene.

A *medium-n analysis* shows my theory well explains all great power rivalries
since 1850. I trace my causal mechanism through a detailed historical analysis
of *Anglo-Soviet relations at the origins of the Cold War*. I gain an
insider-view of how intelligence analysts estimate threats with an *elite (CIA,
DoD staff) war game survey experiment.* I animate a theoretically sophisticated
defense of *America's reaction to China's rise* in the post-Cold War era with
*100s of Washington-insider interviews* including NSC staff and former CIA
Deputy Directors.
:::

</details>

<details class="book-cta">
<summary>BibTeX</summary>

```{=html}
<pre>@book{joseph2026origins,
  author    = {Joseph, Michael F.},
  title     = {The Origins of Great Power Rivalries},
  publisher = {Cambridge University Press},
  address   = {Cambridge},
  year      = {2026}
}</pre>
```

<a class="bib-dl" href="files/bibtex/book.txt" download>Download .txt</a>

</details>

[Book website](https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/origins-of-great-power-rivalries/3CAD163C06975E7FBB48C6112C9B63D3?utm_campaign=shareaholic&utm_medium=copy_link&utm_source=bookmark){.cta-link}

:::
::::::

## Published Articles

```{r, results='asis', echo=FALSE}
render_pubs("published")
```

<!-- ## Policy & Commentary

```{r, results='asis', echo=FALSE}
render_pubs("policy_commentary")
```
--> 

## Select Working Papers

```{r, results='asis', echo=FALSE}
render_pubs("working_papers")
```





 
## Research Grants \& Fellowships


#### Grants
- National Science Foundation Security \& preparedness Program. \$450,000.
- Sar \& Britta Levitan Endowmen. \$20,000
- Herbst Family Graduate Endowment. \$20,000.
 

#### In-Residence Research Fellowships

- Australian National University, Research School of Social Sciences, In-Residence Fellowship.

- Bradley Fellow in Foreign Policy.
